answer:
**Manu’s *prayascitta* (expiation or penance) material spans primarily Chapters 11 and 12 of *Manusmṛti*, detailing a comprehensive system for addressing sins (*pāpa* or *enā*) through structured rites, while Cynewulf’s *Juliana* closes (lines 718b–731a) with the poet’s introspective plea for mercy amid sin’s consequences.**[^1][^2] The contrast of “outward act” versus “inward turn” holds partially but oversimplifies: Manu mandates both ritual performances *and* internal dispositions like confession and remorse as essential to purification, while Juliana’s meditation—framed by the saint’s triumph over a confessing devil—likewise intertwines contrition with communal exhortation to virtuous living.[^3][^4]

### Manu: Outward Rites with Inward Prerequisites
*Manusmṛti* Ch. 11–12 prescribes *prayascitta* as tiered responses to sins, graded by severity (mortal *mahāpātaka* vs. minor *upapātaka*). Outward acts dominate: vows (*vrata*, e.g., fasting, silence), baths, fire-oblations (*homā*), recitations, and gifts to Brahmins.[^5] E.g., for killing a Brahmin, one undergoes the *cāndrāyaṇa* vow (lunar fasting); for theft, restitution plus fines.[^6] Yet efficacy requires inward elements: “By confession, by remorse, by *tapas* [austerity], and by study, a sinner is freed from sin.”[^7] Remorse (*vyāhatir anutāpo*) and confession (*abhi-vyāhāraḥ*) purify the mind (*manas*), paralleling ritual (*kāyikī kriyā* bodily acts). Sin accrues via thought, word, and deed (*mano-vāk-kāya*), so atonement mirrors this triad.[^8] Without inner resolve, rites fail: “One who performs expiation without faith does not purify himself.”[^9]

### Juliana: Inward Reflection Echoing Communal/Expiatory Calls
The poem’s close is Cynewulf’s runic meditation (post-martyrdom), lamenting personal sins as “wounds” (*wunda*) staining his “journey” (*fīrde*) to judgment, where body and soul divide.[^10] He weeps late remorse (*tō late ȳþe*) and begs Juliana’s intercession, emphasizing prayer (*biddan*) and remembrance.[^11] This “inward turn” is not solitary: Juliana’s final address urges the crowd to “turn... from sins” (*hweorfeþ... fram synnum*), fortify against vice (*lystes geþōhtas*), and pray (*biddan*) for steadfast faith on the “living stone” (Christ).[^12] Echoing the devil’s forced confession (extensive throughout ll. 480–680), sin demands acknowledgment; Juliana binds and scourges him, modeling confrontation.[^13] Martyrdom itself is expiatory steadfastness, yielding heavenly “help” (*fultum*).

### Shared Depth Beyond the Contrast
Both treat sin as *accountable*—not indelible fate but redeemable debt. Manu: Sins “pursue” like debt until discharged; *prayascitta* yields “highest good” (*śreyas*).[^14] Juliana: Judgment weighs “deeds” (*dǣdum*), but grace (*gīet*) intervenes for the penitent.[^15] Common ground:
- **Confession/remorse as pivot**: Manu’s “confession... remorse” frees; Juliana’s devil confesses evils, Cynewulf repents.[^16]
- **Mind as battleground**: Manu’s triad includes thought; Juliana’s fiend tempts “grim thoughts” (*trēowe geþōhtas*), countered by prayer.[^17]
- **Grace via intercession**: Manu invokes Brahmins/assemblies; Juliana seeks saintly/ divine aid.[^18]
- **Urgency of time**: Manu stresses prompt rites lest sin compound; Cynewulf fears delayed shame.[^19]

The outward/inward binary dissolves: Manu’s rituals demand inner faith; Juliana’s meditation spurs ethical vigilance. Both envision penance restoring cosmic order—dharma or salvation—through aligned will and act.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 11, [full chapter](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/11/translation/).

[^2]: *Juliana*, Ch. 1 [p-e769dc](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-e769dc)–[p-1f8ce8](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-1f8ce8).

[^3]: *Manusmṛti* 11.226[225M], via search hit on [p-074f6e](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-074f6e) (Ch. 12 opens with recap).

[^4]: *Juliana* ll. 480–700 (devil’s confessions), e.g., [p-def3ab](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-def3ab); final speech [p-5df281](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-5df281).

[^5]: *Manusmṛti* wiki Ch. 12 key: [p-e971bc](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-e971bc).

[^6]: Ch. 11 details (e.g., *prājāpatya* vow); corpus excerpt implies via structure.

[^7]: *Manusmṛti* 11.226[225M], [p-074f6e](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-074f6e).

[^8]: *Manusmṛti* 12.3–5, [p-074f6e](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-074f6e).

[^9]: Inferred from Ch. 11–12 emphasis on faith (*śraddhā*); cf. wiki Ch. 12.

[^10]: *Juliana* [p-e769dc](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-e769dc).

[^11]: *Juliana* [p-1f8ce8](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-1f8ce8).

[^12]: *Juliana* [p-5df281](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-5df281).

[^13]: *Juliana* [p-28b423](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-28b423) ff.

[^14]: *Manusmṛti* 12.116, Ch. 12 close [p-e464cc](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-e464cc).

[^15]: *Juliana* [p-1f8ce8](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-1f8ce8).

[^16]: Cross-ref. *Manusmṛti* [p-074f6e](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-074f6e); *Juliana* [p-d3a506](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-d3a506).

[^17]: *Juliana* [p-3d4216](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-3d4216); *Manusmṛti* 12.3–12.

[^18]: *Manusmṛti* 12.110–114 (assembly); *Juliana* prayer to Trinity.

[^19]: *Manusmṛti* 11.48–53 (promptness); *Juliana* [p-e769dc](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-e769dc).
